authoritative_sources.title: About Us
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public manufacturing, quality, or food-safety standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: IAOB is an official IATF oversight office that publicly states it oversees and supports consistent application of IATF 16949 to improve quality and delivery performance in the global automotive supply chain.
used_for: why automotive quality-system boundaries are authoritative for this lane
separating operations execution from formal quality-system approval
grounding adjacent-lane routing in the IATF ecosystem

authoritative_sources.title: Performance Complaints
authoritative_sources.source_class: Release, hold, or nonconformance operating references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: IAOB's public performance-complaint workflow is authoritative for when delivery, quality, or process failures under an IATF-governed quality system escalate beyond routine internal handling.
used_for: quality or delivery failure boundary conditions
routing from operations into formal quality escalation

authoritative_sources.title: MMOG/LE Work Group
authoritative_sources.source_class: Supplier, traceability, or inventory-control workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AIAG states that MMOG/LE is a global best-practice standard jointly managed with Odette to assess materials-management capability, making it authoritative for automotive inventory, logistics, and materials workflow expectations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Materials readiness, inventory control, and execution prerequisites
used_for: authoritative automotive terminology for flow and control

authoritative_sources.title: Guidelines for an Automotive Supply Chain Management
authoritative_sources.source_class: Supplier, traceability, or inventory-control workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: AIAG's GMMLA overview identifies the publication as operational guidelines for a materials-management and logistics agreement between parties, making it a recognized automotive reference for inventory, supplier, and logistics workflow discipline.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Cross-party automotive materials and logistics coordination
used_for: boundary between execution handling and cross-party approval

authoritative_sources.title: Track & Trace
authoritative_sources.source_class: Supplier, traceability, or inventory-control workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Odette is a recognized automotive supply-chain standards body. Its public Track & Trace resources are authoritative for common terminology covering labels, inventory, transport status, event capture, and cross-system traceability.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Cross-system event normalization and traceability-aware execution
used_for: event and status handling across plant, warehouse, and logistics contexts

authoritative_sources.title: Traceability of Vehicle Components
authoritative_sources.source_class: Supplier, traceability, or inventory-control workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Odette's component-traceability publication is an authoritative automotive operating reference for standardizing tracking processes and technical specifications across organizations and systems.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Component genealogy, lot identity, and cross-organization evidence handling

authoritative_sources.title: VIN Labels in the Vehicle Distribution Process
authoritative_sources.source_class: Supplier, traceability, or inventory-control workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This Odette and ECG recommendation is an authoritative public operating reference for VIN-label location, minimum content, barcode standards, and label quality expectations in automotive handoffs.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Label, scan, and handoff evidence normalization

authoritative_sources.title: What is the GS1 Traceability Standard?
authoritative_sources.source_class: Supplier, traceability, or inventory-control workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GS1 is the recognized global standards body for interoperable supply-chain identifiers and traceability. Its public traceability standard summary defines Critical Tracking Events and Key Data Elements for interoperable traceability systems.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Traceability model design and evidence completeness checking

authoritative_sources.title: What is GS1’s role in traceability?
authoritative_sources.source_class: Supplier, traceability, or inventory-control workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GS1's public traceability guidance is authoritative for how master, transactional, and event data should be identified, captured, shared, and interpreted across the end-to-end supply chain.
used_for: master, transactional, and event data categories

authoritative_sources.title: 49 CFR Part 565 - Vehicle Identification Number (VIN) Requirements
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public manufacturing, quality, or food-safety standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: 49 CFR Part 565 is the current federal rule for VIN format, content, plant-of-manufacture coding, and production-sequence identity, making it authoritative for VIN-level traceability records.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: VIN identity and authoritative record validation

authoritative_sources.title: VIN Decoder
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public manufacturing, quality, or food-safety standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NHTSA's VIN decoder is the public agency operating reference for decoding manufacturer-reported VIN data, including build-plant information used in downstream operations and logistics checks.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Runtime VIN lookup and identity confirmation

authoritative_sources.title: 49 CFR Part 573 - Defect and Noncompliance Responsibility and Reports
authoritative_sources.source_class: Release, hold, or nonconformance operating references
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: 49 CFR Part 573 is the current federal rule for defect and noncompliance reporting, recall campaign documentation, and sale or lease prohibitions, making it authoritative for release-boundary and escalation handling.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Recall, hold, noncompliance, and release-boundary handling
used_for: refusal of unsupported release or completion claims

workflow_stages.name: intake and prerequisite validation
workflow_stages.description: Capture request scope, plant and line context, production order, lifecycle stage, systems of record, and tenant authority rules before any execution-state change is recommended.
source_classes: Public manufacturing, quality, or food-safety standards
Supplier, traceability, or inventory-control workflow guidance
Release, hold, or nonconformance operating references

workflow_stages.name: materials, traceability, and readiness confirmation
workflow_stages.description: Reconcile lot, batch, VIN, supplier, inventory, sequence, and handoff evidence across ERP, MES, WMS, supplier, and logistics systems before work advances.
source_classes: Supplier, traceability, or inventory-control workflow guidance
Public manufacturing, quality, or food-safety standards

workflow_stages.name: in-process execution and lifecycle-state progression
workflow_stages.description: Advance, hold, or rework accepted work based on documented execution state, shortage posture, operator handoff evidence, and nonconformance signals without absorbing approval authority.
source_classes: Supplier, traceability, or inventory-control workflow guidance
Release, hold, or nonconformance operating references

workflow_stages.name: exception, supplier, and release-boundary escalation
workflow_stages.description: Stop routine execution when traceability conflicts, quality holds, supplier substitution pressure, or recall or noncompliance boundaries change whether work may continue.
source_classes: Release, hold, or nonconformance operating references
Public manufacturing, quality, or food-safety standards
Supplier, traceability, or inventory-control workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: completion evidence and safe closure
workflow_stages.description: Document completion only when the closure checklist, scan evidence, inventory and traceability state, and release-dependent artifacts are explicit and auditable.
source_classes: Supplier, traceability, or inventory-control workflow guidance
Release, hold, or nonconformance operating references

artifacts: production orders, build slots, travelers, operator instructions, and completion checklists
batch or lot records, VIN or serial genealogy, scan events, and traceability logs
inventory states, line-side replenishment records, warehouse picks, dock receipts, and shortage logs
supplier records, approved-source constraints, ASNs, substitution requests, and controlled-shipping signals
quality holds, nonconformance reports, quarantine records, containment records, and release records
change-control documentation, rework authorizations, handoff notes, and escalation packets

allowed: classify requests as in-scope, blocked, on hold, rework, or complete for automotive operations execution
request or validate the minimum tenant facts, authoritative records, and source context required to continue safely
prepare evidence-backed execution status updates, completion records, readiness summaries, and escalation packets without overstating authority
advance, hold, rework, or complete work only when documented execution state and evidence support that disposition
escalate: final quality disposition, quality-release decisions, or hold-release overrides
engineering deviations, formal change-control approval, or safety-risk acceptance
supplier approval, supplier substitution approval, or controlled-shipping exit approval
legal or regulatory interpretation, recall authority, or executive policy override
refuse: continue or complete work when required plant, lot, inventory, supplier, or release records are missing and would change the outcome
mark work complete without scan, closure, or release-dependent evidence
bypass traceability, hold, release, or quality controls to preserve throughput or launch timing

domain_failure_modes: traceability breaks between ERP, MES, WMS, and supplier records allow the wrong lot, VIN, or part population to advance
held or quarantined product is treated as releasable because completion pressure overrides formal quality boundaries
supplier substitutions or containment exits are treated as routine execution decisions instead of procurement or supplier-quality approvals
inventory, sequence, and dock-to-line conflicts cause throughput loss or misbuild because authoritative records were not reconciled
work is closed without completion evidence, leaving an unauditable completion record
public recall or noncompliance boundaries are overridden by local policy or urgency
the lane drifts into engineering, quality, supplier, or legal authority because the request originated in an execution queue

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Supplier or substitution decision outside lane authority
Execution exception that requires rework or escalation
Request to mark work complete without evidence of completion

unresolved_ambiguities.details: Public sources define the boundary that held or recall-affected work must not be released casually, but each tenant still must supply local hold codes, disposition workflows, and named release owners.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: plant-specific completion evidence and scan discipline
unresolved_ambiguities.details: Automotive plants differ on traveler shapes, dual-scan expectations, closure checklist steps, and whether VIN, lot, or serial evidence is required at each stage.
Public sources support escalation and refusal boundaries, but approved-source lists, containment-exit rules, and authorized substitution logic must be injected by the tenant.
